analogy: Think of "Carbon and Its Compounds" through the chapter's own opening images: Science 58 Carbon and its Compounds 4 CHAPTER In the last Chapter, we came to know many compounds of importance to us. In this Chapter we will study about some more interesting compounds and their properties. Also, we shall be learning about carbon, an element... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Carbon and Its Compounds is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that science 58 Carbon and its Compounds 4 CHAPTER In the last Chapter, we came to know many compounds of importance to us.

The text explains that in this Chapter we will study about some more interesting compounds and their properties.

The text explains that also, we shall be learning about carbon, an element which is of immense significance to us in both its elemental form and in the combined form.

The text explains that things made Things made Others of metal of glass/clay Activity 4.1 Activity 4.1 Activity 4.1 Activity 4.1 Activity 4.1 n Make a list of ten things you have used or consumed since the morning. n Compile this list with the lists made by your classmates and then sort the items into the adjacent Table. n If there are items which are made up of more than one material, put them into both the relevant columns of the table.

The text explains that look at the items that come in the last column of the above table filled by you – your teacher will be able to tell you that most of them are made up of compounds of carbon.

The text explains that what would be the product if a compound containing carbon is burnt?

The text explains that food, clothes, medicines, books, or many of the things that you listed are all based on this versatile element carbon.

The text explains that in addition, all living structures are carbon based.

The text explains that the amount of carbon present in the earth’s crust and in the atmosphere is quite meagre.

The text explains that the earth’s crust has only 0.02% carbon in the form of minerals (like carbonates, hydrogen- carbonates, coal and petroleum) and the atmosphere has 0.03% of carbon dioxide.

The text explains that in spite of this small amount of carbon available in nature, the importance of carbon seems to be immense.

The text explains that in this Chapter, we will know about the properties of carbon which make carbon so important to us.

The text explains that 4.1 BONDING IN CARBON – THE COVALENT BOND In the previous Chapter, we have studied the properties of ionic compounds.

The text explains that we saw that ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points and conduct electricity in solution or in the molten state.

The text explains that carbon and its Compounds 59 saw how the nature of bonding in ionic compounds explains these properties.

The text explains that let us now study the properties of some carbon compounds.

The text explains that diamonds can be synthesised by subjecting pure carbon to very high pressure and temperature.

The text explains that these were crushed into the earth, perhaps by earthquakes or volcanic eruptions.

The text explains that they were pressed down by layers of earth and rock.

The text explains that bacteria attacked the dead remains, turning them into oil and gas under the high pressures they were being subjected to.

The text explains that meanwhile, the silt was slowly compressed into rock.

The text explains that the oil and gas seeped into the porous parts of the rock, and got trapped like water in a sponge.

The text explains that science 72 4.4.1 Properties of Ethanol Ethanol is a liquid at room temperature (refer to Table 4.1 for the melting and boiling points of ethanol).

The text explains that however, unlike mineral acids like HCl, which are completely ionised, carboxylic acids are weak acids.

The text explains that science 74 CH COOC H C H OH+CH COONa 3 2 5 2 5 3 NaOH (ii) Reaction with a base: Like mineral acids, ethanoic acid reacts with a base such as sodium hydroxide to give a salt (sodium ethanoate or commonly called sodium acetate) and water: NaOH + CH3COOH → CH3COONa + H2O How does ethanoic acid react with carbonates and hydrogencarbonates?

The text explains that thus, clusters of molecules in which the hydrophobic tails are in the interior of the cluster and the ionic ends are on the surface of the cluster.

The text explains that these carbon chains may be in the form of straight chains, branched chains or rings. n The ability of carbon to form chains gives rise to a homologous series of compounds in which the same functional group is attached to carbon chains of different lengths. n The functional groups such as alcohols, aldehydes, ketones and carboxylic acids bestow characteristic properties to the carbon compounds that contain them. n Carbon and its compounds are some of our major sources of fuels. n Ethanol and ethanoic acid are carbon compounds of importance in our daily lives. n The action of soaps and detergents is based on the presence of both hydrophobic and hydrophilic groups in the molecule and this helps to emulsify the oily dirt and hence its removal.

The text explains that explain the formation of scum when hard water is treated with soap.

The text explains that what change will you observe if you test soap with litmus paper (red and blue)?

The text explains that which of the following hydrocarbons undergo addition reactions: C2H6, C3H8, C3H6, C2H2 and CH4.

The text explains that give a test that can be used to differentiate between saturated and unsaturated hydrocarbons.

The text explains that explain the mechanism of the cleaning action of soaps.

The text explains that i Use molecular model kits to make models of the compounds you have learnt in this Chapter.

The text explains that iI n Take about 20 mL of castor oil/cotton seed oil/linseed oil/soyabean oil in a beaker.

The text explains that heat the mixture with continuous stirring for a few minutes till the mixture thickens.

The text explains that stir the mixture well and allow it to cool. n You can cut out the soap in fancy shapes.

The text explains that you can also add perfume to the soap before it sets.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 4: Carbon and Its Compounds

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
